An author asked my client for a book endorsement, to print alongside others. Unprompted inbound, the kind consistent authority-building creates.

05 // Built and coded by me

Orbit: the ops system I made to run all of this —

I designed and coded Orbit end to end: a content calendar for podcast, X and LinkedIn; an ICP radar that finds people posting about our keywords; an AI content generator; a docs hub; and a leads database. I don't just write content. I build the machine that runs it.

Content calendar across podcast, X and LinkedIn for multiple team members, color-coded by person and platform.
Toolbox: in-house content AI and lead intelligence tools.
Content generator: turns blogs and links into posts and newsletters.
ICP Radar: scans LinkedIn for people posting about our target keywords.
Leads database: daily qualified-lead archives, exportable to CSV.

I studied your content first

Two things I'd fix in week one

01
I'd write your posts as personal stories, not CRO reports.
Your data earns trust, but story earns attention. I'd write your wins as first-person narratives: the client who almost walked, the test that humbled you, the call that changed your mind. Same insight, wrapped in a moment people feel and want to share. Personalized to you, in your voice, so it reads like you talking, not a case study.
02
Carousels are an open lane for you.
Your A/B results and before-and-after CRO wins are visual by nature. One weekly carousel turns a test result into a swipe and adds an impression layer with no new content created.

Written in your voice

Two posts, written as your stories

Personal, first-person, built to be shared. Your CRO thinking, told the way you'd actually tell it.

LinkedIn · Personal story

I said no to a founder who tried to hand me a bigger budget last week.

He came in ready to double his ad spend.

Sales had been flat for two months, and more traffic felt like the obvious fix.

But something just didn't feel right.

So before he spent a cent, I asked to see his analytics.

The ads were fine.

People were clicking, landing on the site, having a look around, they just weren't buying.

He was about to pay to send even more people into a bucket with a hole in the bottom.

So we left his budget alone and spent a week patching the three spots where people kept slipping away.

He had the same traffic, it just finally started converting.

Sales moved more in that one week than in the two months before it.

More traffic is the loud answer, but most of the time the quiet one wins.

The money is already on the table.

Your job is to stop it walking out the door.

LinkedIn · Personal story

I've failed the same gym move about 200 times.

Some days it looks like nothing is happening.

I just show up, do the reps, go home.

No breakthrough, no big moment.

Then one morning you hold a position that felt impossible back in spring, and it hits you that all those boring days were doing something after all.

CRO works the exact same way.

People want one dramatic redesign that fixes everything by Friday.

What actually moves things is small and a little dull.

A clearer headline, one less step at checkout.

A button people can find without hunting for it.

None of it feels impressive the day you ship it.

But you test it, keep what works, run it again.

A few months later the brand is making far more from the same traffic, and you can't point to the one change that did it.

It was all of them.

The redesign gets the applause, but the small reps are what quietly stack up.

Same in the gym, same on a website.

Ready when you are

If this is the operator you wanted, let's talk.

No deck, no long proposal. Just a quick call to see if we fit.
